generalities.text: Main THE ENCYCLOPEDIA OF PURE MATERIA MEDICA By TIMOTHY F. ALLEN, A.M., M.D. Presented by Médi-T KALI PICRICUM. Potassium picrate (or picro-nitrate), C 6 H 2 K(NO2)3O. Authority. 1 , Wolf and Gouzee, Zeit. f. hom. Kl., 1 No. 7, constant pathogenetic effects when administered for intermittent fever. ► Icterus ; diarrha; pain in the pit of the stomach; violent eructations; colic; urine clear, brownish-red, not containing any coloring matter from the bile, but large quantities of urate of ammonia. [1] . Copyright © Médi-T ® 2000 Main

keynotes.text: Allen quotes from Wolff and Gouzee the following constant pathogenetic effects observed in patients to whom K. pi. was given for intermittent fever: jaundice; diarrhœa; pain in pit of stomach; violent eructations; colic; urine clear, brownish red, not from bile pigment, but from large quantities of urate of ammonia.─K. pi. is an intensely yellow salt, and yellow substances generally act powerfully on the liver, as is the case with Picric acid itself and its other salts.

_raw: Compare: Pic. ac., Fe. pi. Copyright © Médi-T ® 2000 Main
